data.title: Verizon: Tariff Panic Offers Attractive Setup For An Option Play
data.description:
VZ stock: implied volatility hovers near 52-week high

My last analysis on Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ)(NEOE:VZ:CA) was published more than a month ago. That article was titled “Verizon Q4: Let Your Profit Run”. As the title hinted, the article served as a review for its Q4 earnings report (ER) and gave the stock a buy rating. Quote:

Since my last writing, Verizon has reported strong Q4 earnings. But its stock price advanced even more, resulting in a P/E expansion to 9.09x (from 8.5x at the time of my last writing). A reexamination of the stock shows that the current valuation is still attractive, with a risk premium at a multi-year low. Furthermore, VZ's dividend yield of 6.38% is compelling, with a payout ratio of 59.45%, leaving ample room for future growth and downside protection.

Since that writing, it feels like an understatement to say that there have been some crucial developments both in terms of macroeconomics and geopolitics as a result of the tariff conflicts. Against this macroscopic backdrop, the thesis of this article is to explain why I believe the option market is overestimating the uncertainties facing VZ. As a result, I see an attractive setup for investors to sell options for at least two reasons.

First, the implied volatilities (IV) underlying VZ options are near their 52-week peak level as you can see from the following screenshot taken from MarketChameleon. To wit, the table shows that the current IV of VZ is about 31.4, placing it in the 98% percentile rank in the past 52 weeks. In other words, its IV was lower than the current level 98% of the time in the past year. Moreover, the current IV of 31.4 is also substantially above its 20-day HV (aka historical or realized volatility) of 25.8 by more about 22%. Such an extreme IV makes pricing very favorable for option sellers (and I will revisit this later).

Second, I can see why the ongoing tariff situation poses extreme uncertainties for the overall market. But I think such an extreme volatility, as reflected in VZ’s current IV, is misplaced in the case of VZ. In the next section, I will analyze VZ’s profit exposure and argue why the stock is far less sensitive to higher tariffs rates than the overall economy.

VZ stock: profit exposure

I see a few key factors that make VZ stock far less sensitive to higher tariff rates than the overall economy. To start with, the company is primarily a provider of telecommunication services within the United States. Unlike many sectors heavily reliant on international trade and global supply chains (like manufacturing or retail), 100% of Verizon's revenue is domestic in recent years as you can see from the following chart.

Of course, the fact that its revenues are 100% generated domestically does not mean it is completely immune to higher tariffs. The company still needs to purchase equipment (some of which could be imported) and thus could be indirectly impacted by tariff changes. That is why we need to look further into the breakdown of its profit composition. The bar chart shown below serves this purpose by illustrating Verizon’s revenue exposure across various segments. As seen, the largest portion of revenue (more than a half at 57.04%) is derived from Consumer Service. The segment that is most likely to feel some impact from high tariffs is its Consumer Wireless Equipment in my model. But this segment contributes a relatively minor 14.54% to the total revenue only.

VZ stock: specific option ideas

Due to this above gap between the extreme IV and its relatively low sensitivity to tariffs, I think the current option premiums are attractively priced for sellers. To wit, the following option chain shows some specific options ideas for VZ (both put and call options) with an expiration date of July 18, 2025. I did not randomly pick this date. It is bit more than 90 days from now, the grace period that the U.S. has just granted to its trading partners (except China). Thus, options with this expiry could help sellers to maximize the benefits from the extreme volatility during this period.

Now, let’s look at a particular example, an NTM put option (near-the-money) with a strike of $44. This option last traded at a price of $2.45 as seen. With the stock price as of this writing ($43.6), this premium translates into a return of more than 5.6% in about 90 days. Thus, the extreme IV could help put sellers to either get the shares at lower effective prices or earn a pretty attractive premium income (more than 20% when annualized). Similarly, existing shareholder can consider the use of covered-call options to significantly boost the income on their VZ shares. Assuming the options are not exercised before expiry, call sellers can keep both the option premium and also collect the dividend payouts in the meantime (VZ currently offers a FWD yield of 6.3% as of this writing).

Other risks and final thoughts

Before concluding, let me point out some risks - both in the positive and also negative directions - entailed by my forgoing analysis. Before anything else, a strong caution is in order for investors new to options. Trading options are more complicated than trading shares directly, and the loss can be unlimited for option sellers (either put or call).

In terms of business fundamentals, the company is facing some uncertainties. First, the company has a sizable pending acquisition (of around $20 billion) of Frontier Communications. Frontier is the largest pure-play fiber Internet provider in the United States. I expect the consummation of this acquisition to take time and also likely increase costs in the near term before the synergistic benefits materialize for the long term. Speaking of longer term, I feel quite positive of the prospects here. As an example, the consensus EPS estimates project 4.1% EPS growth rate for the next 5 years as seen. Do not be under-impressed by such a rate. It is already sufficient to make the growth-adjusted valuation very attractive. As a textbook dividend stock, let use the following PEGY ratio that Peter Lynch promoted to illustrate this point.

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

For VZ under current conditions, with a FWD P/E of 9.2x, a growth estimate of 4.1% CAGR, and a dividend yield of 6.3%+, the PEGY ratio is substantially below the ideal 1x threshold.

To close, my thesis in this article is twofold. The use options is part of the investment method used in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) for hedging, income generation, and also leveraging purposes. In the case of VZ under current conditions, we feel the tariff panic has provided an attractive set up for its use given the discrepancy between the extreme IV of its options and its relatively low sensitivity to tariff rate changes. Finally, a word about the rating and also the timeframe this article designates to VZ. First, given that my thesis is based on the discrepancy I see in VZ’s IV and is applicable to both call and put options. As such, the rating I picked for this article is a hold. Second, this thesis is only meant for the near term (say the next ~90 days or so) for the tariff negations to work out.

data.title: Community Financial System: Shares Look Cheap On Margin Expansion Potential
data.description:
As regular readers will know, I've been covering a few smaller, off-the-beaten-path bank stocks recently. These banks typically have a few things in common: they source cheaper-than-average funding; have a history of below-average loan losses; and, perhaps most interesting of all, they have a long track record of delivering consecutive annual dividend hikes, including throughout the last financial crisis.

I am going to round this unofficial series off with a look at Community Financial System (NYSE:CBU). This northeastern bank ticks all of the boxes above, but its stock has struggled quite a bit this year, falling by around 16% on the back of tariff fears.

That move has left Community trading for less than 15x last year's EPS. While that may not look particularly cheap for a regional bank, 2024 wasn't a very good year for Community, and earnings normalization would drive that multiple quite a bit lower. On that basis, I think the shares are decent value right now, and I rate the stock a 'Buy'.

An Overview Of Community Financial System

Community Financial System is the holding company of namesake Community Bank, which operates over 100 branches in the Northeast of the country. The majority of its branches are in upstate New York, though it also has a footprint in Pennsylvania, Vermont and Massachusetts. Community had around $16.5 billion in assets, $13.5 in deposits and $10.5 billion in loans at the end of last year.

Community is a very good bank. It has struggled a bit in recent years, but as the chart below shows, its ROA has typically been higher than the average bank.

There are a few things to like about Community. Firstly, it is pretty good at gathering cheap deposits. Around $3.6 billion of these are in non-interest-bearing accounts, roughly equal to 27% of the overall mix. What's more, it was only paying 1.73% on its interest-bearing deposits last year. As you can see, Community has typically funded its interest-earning assets at a much lower rate than the average bank.

Community also appears to be a fairly conservative lender. Most of its loans are in commercial and residential real estate, weighted slightly toward the latter. Around 17% of the book is in Consumer Indirect lending. These are basically automobile loans originated in dealerships rather than in Community bank branches or online, hence being 'indirect'. While this category produces higher losses, Community typically doesn't lose much on the loans it makes overall. In fact, its annual net charge-off rate has historically been well below average. That includes during the last financial crisis, when its net losses peaked at just 24 basis points.

Potential Upside From Margin Expansion

I said in that last section that Community had struggled a bit in recent years. Last year, for example, it made $3.44 per share in net income, which was only around 7% higher than in 2019. At 1.14%, its annual ROA was the second weakest in the past decade.

One particular issue has been the rapid rise in interest rates, with the average annual Federal funds rate rising by 425bps between 2021 and 2024. On the funding side, Community has actually performed very well. Its cost to fund its interest-earning assets only rose by ~30% of the rise in the Fed funds rate, much lower than the average bank.

The issue has been that its asset yield has also lagged significantly. Whereas peers saw a circa 55% earning-asset pass-through rate between 2021 and 2024, Community's was only just over 30%. That's partly down to its loan book; the bank does more residential real estate lending than peers, and these are long-term, fixed-rate loans. It also reflects its securities portfolio, which was still only yielding around 2% last year. As a result, Community's net interest margin was over 30bps below its ten-year average last year. That may not sound like much, but the bank has over $14 billion in interest-earning assets. A move to its ten-year average NIM would add over 25% to last year's EPS.

The good news is that Community still has plenty of scope for asset repricing, even in a lower interest rate environment, so this issue will resolve itself over time. Meanwhile, funding costs have already topped out now that the Fed funds rate has fallen from its peak.

This dynamic also helps put the valuation into its proper context. Community currently trades for $50.40, putting the prior-year P/E ratio at ~14.7x. That doesn't look cheap for a regional bank, that multiple is based on depressed earnings. It would be much cheaper based on the bank's historical average profitability.

Tariffs do present a bit of a curveball. While 2025 and 2026 consensus EPS points to strong growth, it might be prudent to assume downward revisions. The banking industry faces a lot of uncertainty. Compared to how things looked a few months ago, I now expect lower loan growth and higher credit loss provisioning.

However, there could be quite a lot of upsides here if you can afford a 3-5 year timeframe. As the chart above shows, Community's current P/E is low compared to pre-2023 levels, and like I said, that is based on relatively depressed 2024 earnings. In the meantime, the stock offers an okay dividend. The yield is only 3.65%, but it is covered 1.87x by 2024 EPS. The yield is also comfortably above its ten-year average, another sign that the stock could be undervalued.

If you can look past near-term volatility, these shares could offer a nice combination of margin-driven earnings growth, upward P/E re-rating and dividends.

Conclusion

Community Financial System has had a pretty tough year so far, with tariff fears dragging these shares down alongside most of the market. While it has been under-earning recently, this is a pretty good bank once you get under the hood. For investors able to stomach near-term volatility, these shares could offer a compelling combination of earnings growth, P/E expansion and cash dividends.

data.title: Gold ETF Flows: March 2025
data.description:
March and Q1 in review

Global physically backed gold ETFs1 reported strong inflows in March, totalling US$8.6bn (Table 1).2 This helped drive total Q1 flows of US$21bn (226t) to the second-highest quarterly level in dollar terms, only behind Q2 2020's US$24bn (433t).

North America (61%) and Europe (22%) represented the bulk (83%) of net inflows in Q1. Asia contributed 16% – impressive given that the region’s total assets under management (AUM) only account for 7% of the global total. Additionally, first quarter flows in Europe of US$4.6bn stood out as the strongest quarter since Q1 2020. As a result, and aided by gold’s price increase, AUM reached another all-time-high of US$345bn, representing an increase of 13% in March and 28% through the first quarter.

Additionally, collective holdings rose to 3,445t by the end of March, a 92t addition in the month and 226t higher through Q1, reaching the highest month-end level since May 2023 and 470t shy of the record of 3,915t in October 2020.

Regional overview

North American demand led global flows, adding US$6.5bn and constituting 76% of total flows this month, and US$12.9bn during the quarter. This move higher can be attributed to familiar drivers:

  • the strong price momentum sent gold to above the US$3,000/oz threshold3
  • yields remained rangebound
  • the dollar slipped to levels not seen since last November
  • tariff and war uncertainty provided continued support.

Additionally, equity pullbacks, due to growth concerns and market liquidity worries amid ongoing quantitative tightening, further pushed up investor demand for safe-haven assets.4 Also, increased option activity helped drive US$2.1bn (22 tonnes) inflows at monthly expiry.5

As a result, North American funds posted another strong monthly performance, and the region solidified its significant contribution to global quarterly flows.

Europe saw sizable inflows, drawing US$1bn in March and US$4.6bn during Q1. The rally this month stemmed primarily from the UK, Switzerland and Germany. Although the Bank of England made no changes to its benchmark rate during its March meeting, a cloudy growth outlook, further weighed by US tariff concerns, weak stock market performance and the gold price surge, drove demand higher in the UK. Equally, despite a jump in the 10-year German Bund yield in early March amid Germany’s massive spending plan, investors in Europe continue to add gold ETFs to their portfolios as the ECB’s March cut encouraged further easing expectations6 and US tariff risks loom over the growth outlook.

Inflows were sustained for the fourth consecutive month in Asia, attracting nearly US$1bn in March and US$3.3bn through the first quarter. China and Japan dominated demand in March, both likely driven by rocketing gold price performances, which dwarfed other assets in the month, and roaring global trade policy risks. Additionally, inflationary worries may have helped drive gold ETF inflows in Japan. India saw mild outflows, ending its 11-month inflow streak, as investors may have booked profit. Funds in other regions saw another month of positive demand, albeit only modestly at US$98mn, as Australia and South Africa continue to register gold ETF inflows.

Gold ETF holdings and flows by region

Year to date (YTD as of 31 Mar 25)

Monthly (Mar 25)

Quarterly (Q1 25)

Weekly (Week 13 ending 28 Mar 25)

Gold ETF Flows

Weekly

Monthly

Quarterly

Yearly

Demand captures changes in global/regional gold holdings; fund flows capture the net amount of money (in USD) that comes in or out of gold ETFs globally/regionally.

Gold trading volumes pullback

Trading activity across global gold markets in March came in at US$266bn/day – broadly in-line with the quarterly average of US$270bn/day. LBMA OTC trading of US$136bn/day, resulted in a quarterly average of US$140bn/day. This marks a notable increase when compared to the 2024 daily average of US$113bn.

Exchange volumes continued to rise in March, with COMEX taking the charge amid the strong gold price performance. Increased option activity supported North American ETF volumes, but global gold ETF activities still fell mildly m/m.

Total net longs of COMEX’s gold futures fell 3% to 804t by the end of March. Net long positions held by money managers remained relatively stable at 599t, down slightly from 605t at the end of February. While money manager net longs declined during the first half of March—likely due to profit-taking—renewed interest driven by US trade policy and geopolitical uncertainties led to increased exposure later in the month. Notably, this rebound followed five consecutive weeks of de-grossing that began in February, bringing net longs just above year-end levels of 764 tonnes.

Gold ETFs holdings and flows

Year to date (YTD as of 31 Mar 25)

Monthly (Mar 25)

Quarterly (Q1 25)

Weekly (Week 13 ending 28 Mar 25)

*We monitor how fund assets change through time by looking at two key metrics: demand and fund flows.

  • Gold ETF demand is the change in gold holdings during a given period. We use this metric to calculate the quarterly demand estimates reported in Gold Demand Trends.

  • Fund flows represent the amount of money – reported in US dollars – that investors have put into (or retrieved from) a fund during a given period. For more details, see our methodology note.

† 'Global Inflows/positive demand’ refers to the sum of changes of all funds that saw a net increase in holdings over a given period (e.g., month, quarter, etc.). Conversely, ‘global outflows/negative demand’ aggregates changes from funds that saw holdings decline over the same period.

Gold ETFs holdings

Yearly

Quarterly

Monthly

Weekly

Footnotes

  1. We define gold ETFs as regulated securities that hold gold in physical form. These include open-ended funds traded on regulated exchanges and other regulated products such as closed-end funds and mutual funds. A complete list is included in the gold ETF section of Goldhub.com.

  2. We track gold ETF assets in two ways: the quantity of gold they hold, generally measured in tonnes, and the equivalent value of those holdings in US dollars (AUM). We also monitor how these fund assets change through time by looking at two key metrics: demand and fund flows. For more detail, see our ETF methodology note.

  3. You asked, we answered: Gold hits $3,000 – What comes next? | WGC

  4. Bessent says the economy could be ‘starting to roll a little bit’ | CNBC

  5. Monthly option expiry (21 March) drove inflows of US$1.9bn for GLD, and US$60mn for IAU at expiration.

  6. ECB’s Guindos says it’s too soon to predict April rate decision | Bloomberg

Original Post

data.title: Statistics Reveal How Far Behind Meta Is Against Top AI Competitors, It's Not Even Close
data.description:
Meta Platforms’ (NASDAQ:META) key differentiating attributes from the other Mag 7 stocks center around its “open-source” approach, and the fact that it owns some of the most popular social media apps on the planet, paving the path for wide-scale adoption of its AI models and applications. However, this narrative has been crumbling apart lately.

In the previous article back in November 2024, we delved into how Meta had started to benefit from its “open-source” approach as third-party developers continue to extend the capabilities of its Llama AI models. This includes developers from companies like Shopify (SHOP) using distilled versions of Llama for commerce functionalities. I had recommended buying the stock back in November, after which the stock had rallied around 25%, peaking at around $740 in February 2025.

Although since then the stock price has pummeled by 30% from its 52-week high, amid the broader market sell-off. From the rise of DeepSeek’s open-source models to President Trump’s trade war threatening a global recession, the reasons to be bearish have compounded.

To make matters worse, statistics reveal that Meta’s AI assistant “Meta AI” is barely getting any usage traction relative to other prevailing chatbots like ChatGPT, DeepSeek and Gemini, despite possessing the most enviable distribution channels through its suite of popular social media apps like Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp.

META is being downgraded to a “hold” rating.

Meta AI is falling behind competitors

“Meta AI” is the company’s own generative AI-powered chat assistant, to which users can ask questions about any topic and even prompt to generate images. It is Meta Platforms’ response to the rise of new-age AI chatbots like ChatGPT and Claude.

Web analytics platform ‘Similarweb’ revealed that Meta’s AI chatbot only had 245,000 daily consumer traffic in March 2025.

That is barely challenging OpenAI’s lead with 146 million daily visits, and is also behind open-source competitor DeepSeek at 16 million , and chief advertising rival Google’s Gemini assistant at 11 million. In fact, that is even behind Elon Musk’s Grok chatbot, despite the controversy around his political affairs lately.

Now, the bulls may argue that these numbers from Similarweb are simply estimates, and that the exact usage volume for “Meta AI” could be hard to gauge given that it is not a standalone app the way other competing chatbots are.

On the Q4 2024 Meta Platforms earnings call, CFO Susan Li proclaimed that Meta AI had now reached “700 million monthly actives”. However, while Meta’s executives are still talking about monthly users, OpenAI is boasting about Weekly Average Users (WAUs), highlighting earlier this month that ChatGPT had achieved a milestone of 500 million WAUs in March 2025.

So despite Meta boasting 3.3 billion people using at least one of its social media apps every day, it has barely been able to induce greater adoption of its Meta AI assistant within these apps to formidably challenge OpenAI, and other top players.

Furthermore, when CEO Mark Zuckerberg was asked by an analyst on the last earnings call about the monetization pathway for the Meta AI assistant, he essentially ruled out 2025 (emphasis added):

This year my kind of expectation and hope is that we will be at a sufficient scale and have sufficient kind of flywheel of people using it and improvement from that, that this will have a durable advantage. But that doesn't mean that it's going to be a major contributor to the business. … So the actual business opportunity for Meta AI and AI studio and business agents and people interacting with these AIs remains outside of '25 for the most part.

…

There will, I think, be pretty clear monetization opportunities over time, including paid recommendations and including a premium offering, but really not where we are focused in terms of the development of Meta AI today.

Pushing back monetization plans further into the future is certainly not what the market wants to hear right now, with investors eager to see Returns on Investments (ROI) from all the heavy CapEx that Meta Platforms and other major tech companies have embarked on over the past two years.

Nonetheless, Zuckerberg specified a few potential monetization avenues to give us a sense of how revenue could be generated from the Meta AI assistant over time. He mentioned a “premium offering”, which he has talked about on previous earnings calls and entails subscription pricing for Meta’s superior agentic services.

He also referred to “paid recommendations” which is akin to ad placements within users’ Meta AI conversations based on the context of the dialogue.

Zuckerberg also touched upon “AI studio and business agents”, which the company also refers to as “Business AIs” often times. This is an opportunity we have covered in a previous article, whereby Meta would allow businesses to train their own customer service agents on “AI Studio”. They would then be deployed on social media apps like Instagram and Facebook, giving consumers an option to start a conversation with an automated business agent when they are interested in an advertised product.

However, the company has been testing this feature with advertisers for several quarters now, but it still isn’t ready for rollout yet.

Meanwhile, rivals are advancing ahead with offering more commercial experiences through their own generative AI-powered Search and chatbot services.

Last November, Perplexity extended the functionalities of its Search engine to also facilitate shopping activities, in partnership with Shopify. The AI start-up had accumulated 4 million daily users in March 2025 (as per Similarweb data).

Furthermore, OpenAI, which boasts the largest daily user base of 146 million, has also been making noteworthy strides towards facilitating commerce.

In October 2024, OpenAI introduced ChatGPT search, including various search verticals like “Maps” and “Sports”.

OpenAI and Perplexity have also publicly conveyed their ambitions to build their own web browser, taking on Google’s Chrome and Apple’s Safari. Browsers are expected to be the next battlefield as we move onto the agentic phase of this AI revolution.

Moreover, in January 2025 OpenAI introduced “Operator”, which serves as “an agent that can use its own browser to perform tasks”. The company also boasted collaborations with other popular commercial apps amidst the introduction of this AI agent:

Users can personalize their workflows in Operator by adding custom instructions, either for all sites or for specific ones, such as setting preferences for airlines on Booking.com. Operator lets users save prompts for quick access on the homepage, ideal for repeated tasks like restocking groceries on Instacart.

…

We’re collaborating with companies like DoorDash, Instacart, OpenTable, Priceline, StubHub, Thumbtack, Uber, and others to ensure Operator addresses real-world needs - OpenAI

OpenAI’s commercial collaborations essentially overlap with Meta’s ambitions to facilitate similar agentic services for “Meta AI” users going forward.

And this is just the beginning, as these AI start-ups have signaled greater expansion plans to continuously extend the commercial capabilities of their platforms.

Given that ChatGPT already has 146 million daily users, it is certainly a force to be reckoned with. OpenAI is likely to leverage its loyal user base to induce adoption of its other offerings like browsers and agents, subsequently buoying the advertising potency of its platform.

Indeed, the growing popularity of these new-age AI applications offer new avenues for e-commerce and advertising. Consequently, it is prudent to expect the digital advertising market to become more fragmented over time, a market that is currently dominated by Google, Meta and Amazon.

Similar to how investors are uncertain over the market share that Google will be able to command in the Search/ chat assistant market in this generative AI era, we should also be concerned about the Meta’s share of the digital advertising market going forward. As these new-age applications attract more and more users, ad dollars are likely to follow.

Furthermore, it’s not just the advertising side where Meta is likely to face intensifying competition, but also the subscription side.

Numerous AI start-ups have already started offering premium versions of their chatbots on a subscription basis. Recently, Seeking Alpha News publicized data from Earnest Analytics offering insights into the retention rates for the top subscription-based chat assistants on the market.

Below are the aggregated retention rates for the top providers.

The fact that 70% of ChatGPT Plus subscribers keep their paid plans six months after signing up is a notable achievement by OpenAI in its endeavor to build a loyal and monetizable user base.

Likewise, the statistics for other top players like Gemini Advanced and Claude Pro also exhibit how the broader market is making gradual progress in generating revenue from AI-powered chat assistants.

Meanwhile, Meta does not yet offer a subscription-based premium version of its Meta AI assistant. Although in February, CNBC reported that the social media giant plans to launch a standalone app in Q2 2025, and will also start testing a potential “paid subscription”.

Nevertheless, Meta still remains a step behind its top competitors, which have already begun to garner a loyal user base through paid subscriptions and memory features whereby ChatGPT and Gemini Advanced are able to recall past conversations, allowing for more valuable and customized responses to users.

Antitrust litigation threatens the divesture of Instagram and WhatsApp

The fact that Meta now feels inclined to offer a standalone AI app essentially confirms the company’s failure to induce wide-spread usage of its “Meta AI” assistant through its popular social media apps.

Although on the last earnings call, Meta CFO Susan Li did laud the fact that WhatsApp has been the main source of “Meta AI” interactions, followed by Facebook:

So from an app perspective, WhatsApp continues to see the strongest Meta AI usage across our family of apps. People there are using it most frequently for information seeking and educational queries along with emotional support use cases.

…

And on Facebook, which is the second largest driver of Meta AI engagement, we're seeing strong engagement from our feed deep dives integration that lets people ask Meta AI questions about the content that is recommended to that.

However, to make matters worse, Meta Platforms faces antitrust litigation (trial starting on April 14th 2025), with regulators seeking to break up the company through the divesture of Instagram (acquired in 2012) and WhatsApp (acquired in 2014).

Now this would serve as a major blow to the company, given how much Instagram contributes to Meta’s total revenue, and how WhatsApp is currently the most promising channel for “Meta AI” usage and automated business messaging (or “Business AIs”).

Instagram's $32 billion US ad revenue in 2024 represented 48.4% of Meta's total revenue that year, according to Emarketer.

…

WhatsApp's business messaging brought in $1.7 billion in 2024 and grew its business user base in the fourth quarter to more than 530 million daily active users.

– Yahoo Finance

Note, WhatsApp's business messaging mainly derives from countries like India at the moment, where the cost of labor is low. It does not refer to generative AI-powered “Business AIs”.

The possibility of Meta having to break off Instagram and WhatsApp is likely to weigh on stock price performance going forward. And that is on top of the fact that President Trump is keen to sustain the operations of TikTok in the U.S., throwing cold water on the bullish narrative that Meta could benefit from the ban of the Chinese social media app. The Trump administration’s regulatory stance is certainly not in Meta’s favor at the moment.

Upside factors

Considering Meta Platforms from a more positive perspective, it is becoming clearer that the generative AI era will give rise to new form factors for consumer gadgets.

The most promising form factor is “smart glasses”, where Meta Platforms has a lead over other major tech companies.

Our Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses are a real hit, and this will be the year when we understand the trajectory for AI glasses as a category. Many breakout products in the history of consumer electronics have sold 5 million to 10 million units in their third generation. This will be a defining year that determines if we're on a path towards many hundreds of millions and eventually billions of AI glasses

– CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Q4 2024 Meta Platforms earnings call

While the company has not disclosed the exact number of units it has sold so far, the Meta View app (for Ray-Ban smart glasses) and Meta Horizon app (for Quest devices) topped Apple’s App Store download charts during the Holiday season ending 2024.

I've said for a while that I think that glasses are the ideal form factor for an AI device, because you can let an AI assistant on your glasses see what you see and hear what you hear, which gives it the context to be able to understand everything that's going on in your life that you would want to talk to it about and get context on.

– CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Q4 2024 Meta Platforms earnings call

Smart glasses are indeed a promising form factor through which Meta could induce greater usage of “Meta AI”, as it enables users to verbally ask questions about their immediate surroundings, improving the chances of consumers using the company’s generative AI-powered assistant more frequently relative to competitors’ offerings.

But in order for Meta to build a sustainable lead over competitors in this space, it will have to work to turn “Ray-Ban smart glasses” into a break-out product, which Zuckerberg has defined as “5 million to 10 million units in their third generation”. This could become difficult to achieve given the increasing likelihood of a recession this year amid Trump’s global trade war.

The investment case for Meta Platforms

Meta Platforms is due to report first quarter earnings on 30th April 2025.

The market was already disappointed by the guidance it had provided on the previous earnings call, citing foreign exchange headwinds:

We expect first quarter 2025 total revenue to be in the range of $39.5 billion to $41.8 billion. This reflects 8% to 15% year-over-year growth, or 11% to 18% growth on a constant currency basis as our guidance assumes foreign currency is an approximately 3% headwind to year-over-year total revenue growth, based on current exchange rates. This also reflects the effect of lapping leap day in the first quarter of 2024.

– CFO Susan Li, Q4 2024 Meta Platforms earnings call

Since then, President Trump’s trade war has escalated, with global tariffs raising the prices of everything from consumer electronics to groceries. Consequently, consumers are widely expected to cut back on spending, and companies’ advertising budgets could subsequently follow, undermining Meta’s ad revenue growth rates this year.

Hence, the guidance that CFO Susan Li offers on the upcoming earnings call for Q2 2025 and the rest of this year could once again be underwhelming, which is likely to weigh on stock price performance.

Looking out over the longer-term, Meta’s expected EPS FWD Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) is 16.99%, marking a steep drop from the 21% projected around 6 months ago.

While 16.99% is roughly in line with chief advertising rival Google’s anticipated EPS growth rate of 16.26%, it is less than half the projected growth rate of Nvidia at 35.38%.

When we adjust each stock’s Forward PE multiple by these expected EPS growth rates, we obtain the following Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) ratios.

For context, a Forward PEG ratio of 1x would mean that the stock is trading at fair value.

So META trading at 1.21x Forward PEG means it is trading at around a 20% premium to its fair value. It is not unusual for tech stocks to trade at premiums to their fair values, as is the case with Apple and Microsoft.

Key advertising competitor GOOG, on the other hand, appears to be trading at fair value, as the market is reluctant to reward the stock with a premium valuation given that its core Search business faces serious competition from the rise of generative AI-powered chat assistants like ChatGPT and DeepSeek. At the same time, Google also faces the legal requirement to divest its Chrome browser as a result of an antitrust litigation against the company. These major risk factors have led to uncertainty around the company’ future EPS growth potential, hindering the stock from trading at a higher valuation multiple.

This raises the question, can META continue to trade at a premium to GOOG given that it faces a similar plight? As discussed throughout the article, Meta’s own advertising business is also under competitive threat as new-age AI apps gain popularity over “Meta AI”.

And if Google is being required to break off “Chrome”, which is a browser that was built in-house, what are the chances that Meta will evade the prospects of eventually having to divest Instagram and WhatsApp, given that both of these were acquisitions of rising competitors at the time?

While META is not necessarily expensive at this valuation, it’s hard to see the market rewarding the stock with a higher valuation multiple, given that it is lagging top AI competitors in terms of usage volume and monetization, conducive to underwhelming EPS growth for the foreseeable future.

Meta Platforms stock has been downgraded to a ‘hold’ rating.

data.title: Tanger: Irresistible Value On The Dip
data.description:
The stock price of Tanger Inc. (NYSE:SKT) has been through the wringer last week as investors reacted in an extreme fashion to the announcement of new tariffs by the Trump Administration.

That said, during times of distress and heightened emotionality, investors can often find bargains and I think one such bargain is Tanger: the shopping center REIT owns long-term leases, is diversified with real estate spread out over the entire country, has rental upside and sells for compelling valuation multiples, particularly after last week’s market rout.

My last stock classification was ‘Buy’ which is a classification I maintain. I think that passive income investors should use the opportunity of last week’s rout to gobble up shares as the dividend is probably going to go up in 2025.

Tanger still has a growth catalyst (rental rate increases) and should be able to grow its funds from operations vi organic means as well. Tanger also has much better dividend pay-out metrics than its REI peers, and the stock has potential.

Portfolio Anchored In Fast Growing Metro Markets

Tanger has positioned itself as a leading shopping center-focused real estate investment trust with a broad base of operations. As of December 31, 2024, Tanger owned a total of 42 retail shopping centers, reflecting 16.5 million square foot in leaseable, commercial real estate, with the majority of the trust’s portfolio (95%) being open-air. The entire portfolio, has been spread out all over North America, and Tanger is a leading shopping center REIT in its peer group.

Tanger has a relatively big portion of near-term lease expirations, which could be a source of financial strength for the trust in 2025. According to Tanger’s lease maturity schedule, a whopping 23% of its leases are expiring this year.

While some investors might perceive this to be a risk for the real estate investment trust’s cash flow, I’d rather think that the high amount of lease expirations creates an opportunity for Tanger to lock in higher rents on renewal contracts: in 4Q24, Tanger’s rent spread (old rent vs. new rent) was 15%, reflecting 0.6 percentage points QoQ growth.

As I also indicated in my September presentation on Tanger, the shopping center REIT profits from this strong leasing activity, meaning the trust has the opportunity to re-lease expiring commercial real estate space at a higher rate, either to the same tenant, or to a new tenant. The ability to grow rents to lease renegotiations (re-tenanting) is a catalyst for net operating income growth for the trust as well.

Tanger Is Growing Its NOI

Higher rents, strong demand for commercial real estate, particularly in shopping centers and re-tenanting activity, have provided a boost to Tanger’s net operating income.

In 4Q24, Tanger earned $95.2 million in net operating income, up 10% YoY, whereas in 2024 the trust pulled in a total of $365.5 million, up 14% YoY.

Growing net operating income is catalyzed by growing rents, which itself are supported by strong fundamentals in the commercial real estate market. The uptick in rents (spreads) is driving NOI upside for the REIT in 2025.

The retail sales outlook is also moderately good. In December 2024, retail sales grew 0.4% and sales growth was positive in four consecutive quarters. With the U.S. consumer profiting from moderating inflation, the retail sales environment looks good and could potentially support Tanger’s position for upcoming lease negotiations.

Dividend Pay-Out Comparison: Tanger Vs. Major REITs

Tanger is growing its core funds from operations; In 4Q24, Tanger increased its core funds and operations by 4% YoY to $0.54 per share which correspondingly resulted in a rock-solid dividend pay-out ratio of 51%.

In addition, Tanger appears poised to raise its dividend pay-out in 2025, and I think that the shopping center real estate investment trust could possibly give shareholders a 4-5% dividend hike (last year’s dividend hike was 6%).

What follows are Tanger’s updated dividend coverage pay-out metrics for 4Q24:

We can compare Tanger’s dividend pay-out ratio on a last twelve-month basis to the comparable ratios of other REIT peers with both a shopping center and retail focus such as Kimco Realty, Realty Income Corp. (O), National Retail Properties Inc. (NNN). The peer group had an average LTM dividend pay-out ratio for 63% which reflects a 12 percentage point lead over the average on the part of Tanger.

Tanger May Be A Really Good Investment At The Present Price

Tanger is selling for a comparatively high core FFO multiple of 14.2x, but I think the argument could be made that because of the real estate investment trust’s low dividend pay-out ratio (the lowest in the peer group) that the shopping center trust is, from a pay-out angle, a very solid ‘Buy’.

The peer group average FFO multiple is 12.8x, predominantly because Kimco Realty is dragging the average down. Kimco Realty had only a slightly worse LTM dividend pay-out ratio of 59% in 2024.

Retail real estate investment trusts like Realty Income and National Retail Properties sell for higher AFFO multiples due to their lower-risk and better-diversified cash flows.

Realty Income is selling for 13.1x adjusted funds from operations, and we can expect billions of new investments in the next couple of years. National Retail Properties is changing hands for 12.4x and is one of the longest-paying real estate investment trusts in the market.

Why The Investment Thesis Has Downside Risks

There are of course, risks to the investment thesis. In the event that the tariffs that have been announced by the Trump Administration catalyze a recession, retail sales growth might turn negative, undermining my argument about a continuation of NOI growth.

Shopping center real estate investment trusts are cyclical investments, influenced by consumer spending, and they would probably not do well during a recession. Taking into account that Tanger has a peer group-leading dividend pay-out ratio, I think that the dividend is not at risk, however.

My Conclusion

Tanger has a widely-diversified real estate portfolio and has a catalyst from higher rents (re-tenanting of properties) which could equate to rental and net operating income upside in 2025.

Tanger also provides what I perceive to be a very high quality dividend. The shopping center real estate investment trust is also selling at a compelling FFO multiple given the recent market pullback which is an opportunity to buy into the REIT at a much better price.

An investment in Tanger, at a stock price of $30.60 yields 3.60%, and with the dividend more than well-covered by funds from operations, I think passive income investors should be considering Tanger on the dip.

data.title: Missed Meta In 2023? This Might Be Your Second Chance
data.description:
I think the market’s got Meta (NASDAQ:META) wrong right now. The stock’s been knocked down nearly 30% over the past month and while some of that’s tied to broader tech volatility and macro headlines, the core business hasn’t skipped a beat. If anything, it’s firing on more cylinders than ever. Free cash flow hit $52 billion last year, the ad engine is showing real pricing power again, and Meta’s AI and infrastructure bets are scaling fast. But the stock’s trading like it’s stuck in neutral.

At just over $500 a share, Meta is sitting around 20x trailing earnings. That’s low for a company with this level of profitability, balance sheet strength and long-term upside. I don’t think this is just a case of the stock being undervalued—it’s more like the market’s stuck on the wrong narrative. Everyone’s still hung up on Reality Labs losses and regulatory overhangs, but meanwhile, WhatsApp, Threads and Meta AI are slowly becoming real businesses. It’s a disconnect. And for long-term investors, that’s where the edge is.

Why the Market’s Getting This Wrong

Let’s be real—some of the skepticism around Meta isn’t totally crazy. Reality Labs lost $17.7 billion last year and Zuckerberg’s still pouring billions into VR and AR while the payoff feels miles away. But what the market seems to be missing is how insanely strong the Family of Apps business still is. $87 billion in operating income on a 54% margin. That’s not “stable” or “mature”—that’s world-class efficiency.

Even more important: ad impressions are still rising (up 11% in 2024) but now average ad pricing is growing again too (up 10%). That means Meta’s AI-driven ad targeting is working. And it’s happening without the usual reliance on third-party data. The company is figuring out how to make money in a post-ATT privacy-conscious world. That’s huge.

On top of that WhatsApp Business revenue jumped 63% year-over-year. Threads which most people still write off is slowly gaining traction. These aren’t side projects anymore—they’re potential multi-billion-dollar platforms. But the market still treats Meta like a one-trick pony with a high cash burn side hustle.

What the Company’s Actually Saying

Digging into the 10-K management’s message is pretty consistent: we’re not backing off Reality Labs but we’re also not ignoring near-term results. Capex hit $39 billion last year, and they’re guiding $60–$65 billion for 2025—mostly to scale AI infrastructure. That sounds aggressive and it is. But when you’re building your own compute stack models and user endpoints, that spend starts to make sense.

They also called out Meta AI and Llama models more directly this time. These are no longer research toys—they’re being embedded across Instagram, WhatsApp and hardware like smart glasses. If Meta nails this, it could end up with one of the few vertically integrated AI platforms that touches billions of users.

And don’t forget: Meta’s not just spending big—it’s returning capital too. $29.75 billion in buybacks last year and a brand-new dividend. That’s a company confident in its cash flow, not one scrambling to stay relevant.

Valuation Looks Way Too Conservative

This is where things really start to jump out. Meta trades at around 20x trailing earnings despite putting up 38% net margins and nearly $165 billion in revenue last year. EV/EBITDA is hovering around 15x. Compare that to peers: Microsoft’s (MSFT) around 30 earnings, and Amazon (AMZN) is north of 30x. Meta looks underpriced, plain and simple.

If you assume Meta keeps growing the core ad business at a steady 10%, Reality Labs starts to lose less money and free cash flow climbs into the $60–65 range by 2027—which doesn't seem far-fetched given they just did $52 billion last year—you don’t need a crazy multiple to get to $650–$750 per share. That’s 30–50% upside from here. And that doesn’t even bake in a breakout from Threads or a real monetization curve on Meta’s AI tools.

What the Headlines Are Missing

Lately the broader mood around tech has turned sour. You can see it in the headlines: recession talk is back, oil prices are sliding, and Trump’s tariff drama has global markets on edge. Currency volatility in China and a dip in the dollar are just adding fuel to the fire. Everyone’s nervous. And when markets get nervous they start unloading anything with long-dated payoffs—like Meta.

But here’s the thing: none of this has much to do with Meta’s fundamentals. If anything, the company’s insulated by its massive user base and durable ad platform. The news sentiment around Meta specifically? Mostly neutral. Some lawsuits, some product chatter but nothing damaging. Yet the stock’s acting like there was a major miss or guidance cut. There wasn’t.

And while the headlines are screaming about tariff chaos, Meta is launching new versions of its smart glasses at the $1000 price point and scaling AI use across its product suite. That’s not a company pulling back. It’s one that’s doubling down on platform shifts.

Risks Worth Watching

Meta’s got no shortage of risk—some of it baked into the business model, some of it self-inflicted, and a lot of it coming from the outside world. The biggest and most immediate is regulation. Europe, in particular, is hitting hard. The company’s already dealt with a €1.2 billion GDPR fine and had to change how it handles ad targeting across the EU. Between the Digital Markets Act, AI regulation and growing pressure in the U.S., there’s a real chance Meta’s core ad engine gets clipped in key markets.

Then there’s Reality Labs, which is still bleeding cash—$17.7 billion in losses last year alone. Meta keeps insisting this is a long-term play on spatial computing and maybe it is, but there’s no guarantee the bet pays off. A lot is riding on hardware like its upcoming smart glasses. If those flop, it just adds to the skepticism around the entire division.

Content and platform safety are another ongoing battle. Meta’s platforms are massive and messy. There’s no real way to guarantee everything’s clean. The company continues to deal with misinformation, scams, election interference and illegal content slipping through. And now that it’s easing up on content moderation in the name of free expression, that could open it up to new legal headaches.

Cybersecurity is a quieter risk, but no less serious. Meta admits it’s a regular target and that not every hole has been patched. On top of that, it relies on a web of partners and vendors who could become weak links in the chain.

Add in the talent war for AI engineers, exposure to foreign governments, taxes, currency swings and the occasional threat of outright bans—and yeah, there’s a lot going on. But even with all of that, Meta’s still pumping out over $50 billion in free cash flow. The question is whether the market’s overreacting to the noise. Right now, it looks like it might be.

How This Could Play Out

Bear case: In a bear case, Reality Labs keeps bleeding cash with no visible payoff, Threads stays flat or fails and regulators—especially in the EU—keep coming after Meta’s core business. Throw in some macro drag (maybe ad budgets tighten) and earnings miss the low end of estimates. Say EPS drops to around $23. If the market’s feeling sour and only gives that 18x, you’re looking at a stock in the $400–$410 range. Not great, but not a total disaster either—especially considering how much cash Meta still throws off.

Base case: Now, let’s say nothing crazy happens—no wild upside but no disasters either. Ads keep growing at a healthy pace, WhatsApp and Threads add some incremental revenue and AI tools improve margins a bit. Reality Labs continues to lose money but it doesn’t spiral. EPS hits around $29 by 2026, as consensus suggests. Give that a modest 22–25x multiple (not a stretch for a company with 38% net margins), and you’re looking at a price somewhere in the $640–$725 range. That’s a solid return from where the stock sits today.

Bull case: Now here’s where it could really get interesting. If Meta starts unlocking real monetization on Threads, if WhatsApp starts printing meaningful revenue and if Meta AI tools catch on in a meaningful way across both consumer and business apps, then suddenly you’re looking at earnings that blow past current estimates. EPS could push past $35. And if the market rewards that with a 30x multiple? That puts the stock somewhere north of $1,000. That’s a stretch, sure—but it’s not some fantasy moonshot either. Meta has the infrastructure and reach to pull this off if things click.

The base case feels very realistic. And the upside is hard to ignore.

Bottom Line

Meta’s not perfect. It’s spending big, it's taking long-term bets, and it’s facing plenty of heat from regulators and the media. But strip away the noise and you’re left with a company making more money than ever with more levers for growth than it’s had in years—and a stock price that doesn’t seem to reflect any of it.

The last time Meta was this cheap was late 2022, right before it ripped higher. This feels similar. The stock is down not because the business is breaking—but because investors are tired of the story.

That’s usually when things start to get interesting.

My take? Meta’s a Strong Buy. The numbers back it up. The strategy is working. And the market’s not paying attention. That’s exactly when you want to lean in.

data.title: InterDigital: Major Licensing Milestones, But Very Expensive (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
​​InterDigital, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDCC) is a tech company that mainly develops wireless, video, and artificial intelligence [AI] foundational knowledge, standards, and devices. Their work in Internet-of-Things [IoT], edge computing, and optimization has also helped lay the foundation for solutions used in smart cities, homes, and industries. And overall, it’s a fantastic business, but unfortunately, it seems its stock has already priced in a sizeable premium. And, given the current odds of a recession, I feel expensive stocks like IDCC are particularly exposed if there’s a market-wide repricing to lower valuation multiples. So, on balance, I now feel IDCC is a “Hold” for now.

Tech Services And Devices

​InterDigital, Inc. specializes in wireless, video, and artificial intelligence [AI] technologies to produce innovative services and devices. IDCC was founded back in 1972, and it’s currently headquartered in Delaware, USA. As previously noted, their business segments focus on wireless, video, and AI, broadly speaking. The wireless segment has made roughly 4,000 contributions to foundational international standards and has contributed to innovations across several architectures. They’re now focusing on next-generation wireless technologies, such as 5G and 6G. Since my previous article from February 2024, IDCC has made some interesting progress with its IP portfolio, but the stock now does look expensive relative to its potential.

You see, IDCC’s video lab collaborates with other industry bodies to develop video standards, computer graphics, and streaming. Overall, IDCC actually plays a relatively important role in defining and implementing MPEG Immersive Video [MIV] and Extended Reality [XR] for interactive environments. Similarly, their AI lab works on topics that are transversal to wireless and video. Here, IDCC essentially develops solutions that optimize 5G and beyond, which often includes designing video codecs using deep learning algorithms.

IDCC also has some offerings with Internet-of-Things [IoT] devices and technologies. It has contributed to developing core platforms for its connectivity. These systems include 1) low-power communication protocols to allow IoT sensors to run for a long time with small batteries.

Also, they have 2) edge computing for the data to be processed in the device, reducing latency, and 3) dynamic connectivity for 5G to prioritize resources according to their needs. Interestingly, their integration of AI plus IoT can be applied to smart cities. In practical terms, this eventually involves automated control systems that can be used for traffic administration, lighting, and waste management.

This is why IDCC’s contributions are immensely beneficial for smart homes with sensors and appliances so that they can interact with humans. Lastly, the merging of AI and IoT will probably have a great impact on the industrial sector by generating predictive maintenance, with machines automatically reporting issues as they pop up. So all of these factors essentially place IDCC at the foundation of major tech trends.

Major Tech Partnerships

But, more recently, in Q4 2024, IDCC secured several important milestones that give it some interesting strategic momentum. The company reached a new deal with the Oppo Group. The deal basically states that they will use IDCC’s patented wireless, video, and AI technologies.

And it’s worth mentioning that Oppo is one of the largest producers of smartphones in China, with brands like realme and OnePlus. So, with this collaboration, IDCC now has license agreements with the top four cellular phone manufacturers. In other words, they’re now embedded with Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF), and now Oppo. Thus, this basically gives IDCC 70% market coverage in its niche.

Moreover, before this particular IDCC deal with Oppo. These two companies were actually engaged in multiple patent infringement litigations in countries like Germany, the UK, India, and China. This contentious relationship was mostly centered on IDCC’s standard-essential patents [SEPs] related to 3G, 4G, and 5G technologies. So, this agreement also gives IDCC a legal win because these claims are considered dismissed going forward. It basically managed to turn a foe into a friend, which, I think, is, by itself, quite brilliant from management.

Additionally, IDCC renewed its license agreement with ZTE, which is a major telecom and smartphone firm. They had previously signed a deal in 2019 resolving a nine-year patent dispute. And in their latest corporate presentation, IDCC confirmed they renewed this license agreement. It’s also worth mentioning that IDCC also signed the resolution of conflicts with Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) by agreeing to a binding arbitration. This particular arbitration concluded six years of litigation that coincidentally started back in 2019 when IDCC sued Lenovo in the UK High Court for infringement of patents related to 3G and 4G standards.

But probably one of their most important deals happened this week. In April 07, 2025, IDCC announced a multi-year license agreement with HP Inc. (HPQ). This latest deal basically lets HPQ use IDCC’s technologies in its personal computers. Concretely, HP will now be able to incorporate IDCC’s wi-fi and video decoding solutions. And for IDCC, this deal grows its influence in the PC sector, reaching coverage of 50% of the PC global market.

Besides, I believe this only foreshadows even more growth across industries over time, because clearly, IDCC’s technologies are at the core of many potential tech implementations. And on top of that, IDCC recently received the Fierce Network Innovation Award for AI for wireless communications and its contributions to global standards. So, I would say they probably have among the best reputations and competitive edges in this particular industry.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, IDCC trades at a $5.0 billion market cap, making it already one of the largest companies in its niche. Here’s where I feel it’s now time to start being cautious on IDCC. Its balance sheet holds roughly $527.4 million in cash and $430.9 million in short-term investments. This amounts to about $958.3 million in liquidity that they can deploy for their operations, against essentially $471.8 million in debt.

But a quick note on this, $456.3 million of its debt is due within 12 months, so I think a better proxy for their actual liquidity is $486.5 million (assuming they don’t refinance). Lastly, their book value stands at $857.2 million, which does indicate an expensive P/B of 5.8. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is much lower at just 2.7.

Likewise, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on IDCC, they’re projected to generate just $679.5 million in revenues this year. That would also price them at a high P/S of 7.4. Again, their peers’ P/S is much lower at 2.2. In fact, back in my original February 2024 IDCC article, I said my “bull” case for the stock was $203.53 per share. This is why I feel that, at today’s prices, IDCC seems to have already played out its upside potential, as I initially outlined.

So, I think it’s fair to say IDCC is already priced for success, which, I believe, is probably the main drawback with this stock. Other than that, IDCC is actually a cash flow machine at the moment. I estimate they generate in Q4 2024 alone approximately $188.1 million. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX. If we annualize that figure, it would imply IDCC produces almost $752.4 million in positive cash flow every year, which I think is probably its most impressive metric.

But, I still believe that if the economy enters into a recession, stocks that trade at a premium will probably suffer the most. For context, JPMorgan (JPM) maintains a 60% chance of recession despite the pause in the tariffs, so this is no longer an unlikely scenario. And in that sense, IDCC has so far fared relatively well during this latest market pullback, dropping by just 15% so far. Yet, if markets keep dropping, I feel IDCC in particular has much more downside risk at these levels.

Hence, I feel that despite its strong underlying business, the stock itself is probably a “Hold” at these levels for now. Additionally, while they’ve scored some legal wins recently, it’s always possible that some other company might litigate them again. So I do think IDCC investors are still exposed to that type of headline risk, which is definitely heightened due to its currently expensive valuation.

Conclusion: Cautious “Hold” For Now

Overall, I think IDCC has a fantastic business, and it has recently made some important partnerships that only grow its influence across several industries. They’re also a cash flow machine, so I don’t think they’ll have any financing problems for the foreseeable future, and their balance sheet is solid. However, it seems their stock is already a bit too pricey, in particular if the macro environment remains challenging. And if a recession hits, I believe stocks that have a lot of premium embedded into them will probably get hit the worst. Hence, I think on balance, a “Hold” rating makes sense for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Tariff Rollercoaster Continues, ECB Rate Decision And Inflation Data In Focus
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: US Dollar on the ropes following topsy-turvy week

It's been a wild ride to say the least for financial markets this week. Wild swings, multi-year highs and lows being tested, tariffs being pulled back and of course the escalating tensions between the US and China.

The developments this week necessitate us starting with FX markets as the US dollar endured heavy losses, hitting 2022 lows in the process.

The FX market tells an interesting story. Among G10 currencies, only the less-traded Norwegian krone hasn’t moved against the dollar since last Friday. Other currencies have gained, with the pound up 0.8%, the euro, Australian dollar and New Zealand dollar rising 2-3%, and the Swiss franc leading with a 4.5% increase.

Markets are clearly telling us that there is a crisis of confidence in the US dollar at the moment. Whether I believe there should be or not is an entirely different and irrelevant discussion given what we have already seen from markets.

On Thursday, we saw investors move away from US assets. Both stocks and Treasury bonds dropped, even though core inflation came in much lower than expected (0.1% instead of the predicted 0.3% for the month).

Markets are definitely telling us that data is not the main driving force behind market moves. This honor is taken by concern about the combined threat of inflation and growth deceleration.

A perfect example of the challenges facing global markets is reflected in the losses for stock markets around the world. More than $5 trillion in market value has vanished from the MSCI all-country index of world stocks during the roller-coaster ride since April 2.

These developments have helped Gold prices once more, with the precious metal making multiple fresh highs this week. Gold peaked on Friday at around $3245/oz at the time of writing. The precious metal is on course to finish the week around 6.5% up

Oil prices rose on Friday, but both Brent and WTI are set for a second consecutive week of losses. Tariffs being the instigator here as well as recessionary fears are impacting oil demand forecasts just as OPEC + increased output. Earlier today, President Trump's administration said the President wants the US to pump more and more oil, further exacerbating demand and supply dynamics.

US Equity Indexes finished the week on a positive note, with the Dow Jones increasing by 648.65 points (1.64%) to 40,242.31. The S&P 500 went up 93.70 points (1.78%) to 5,361.75, and the Nasdaq climbed 312.92 points (1.91%) to 16,700.23. The week was a positive one for US stocks following President Trump's pause on his 'liberation day tariff agenda'. The move has seen US indexes recover most of the losses which occurred since the April 2 announcement.

The biggest story this week aside from the US dollar comes from the US Treasury market. U.S. 10-year Treasury yields saw their biggest weekly jump in over 20 years on Friday. Rumors of selling, or lack of buying, by foreign investors are adding to concerns over the market with rumors that hedge funds and asset managers were getting margin calls.

Another reason cited for the performance comes through the unwinding of basis trades, a strategy where investors earn from the gap between cash Treasury prices and futures. Many believe this is a large factor behind this week's volatility.

An interesting take from ING THINK caught my attention this week. To summarize, the piece opined that U.S. tariff policies haven’t changed much overall. Here’s why:

Before Trump took office, the average U.S. tariff on imports was 2.5%. By Wednesday morning, with actions like 25% tariffs on metals and autos, and up to 50% on China, that average rose to 28%—the highest since the early 20th century.

Midweek, some countries like the EU and parts of Southeast Asia saw their tariffs drop to 10%. However, China's tariffs, already high at 104%, jumped to 145%.

These adjustments balanced out, leaving the average tariff rate unchanged at 28%.

**Only non-USMCA compliant goods from Canada and Mexico are subject to a 25% tariff. Assumes latest China tariff of 145%, as per latest Bloomberg reports. Colored bars show the contribution to the latest average tariff rate from the tariffs charged on different countries and products.

I found this to be an interesting take and something I had partly anticipated having paid attention to President Trump's previous term. However, it does not hold much weight in the current climate as markets are still moving and that is the reason why we are here after all.

The week ahead: Shortened trading week brings a host of data releases

The upcoming week will focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's plans for new tariffs. Alongside this, markets will also watch U.S. jobs data, an Australian central bank meeting, and a key eurozone inflation report.

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region will be Japanese inflation and a host of data releases from China.

In Japan, inflation is expected to rise, but the Bank of Japan (BoJ) is likely to keep its rate cuts on hold. Goods being shipped early to the US are expected to boost machinery orders and exports.

However, this positive trend might not last beyond the first quarter due to tariff uncertainties. Consumer prices are predicted to go up in March, mainly due to higher service costs. Earlier fresh food price hikes are also expected to show up in eating-out and processed food costs. The BoJ will keep a close eye on how US tariff policies affect the economy while maintaining current rates for now.

It's a busy data week in China as the country grapples with the escalating trade war with the US. None of the tariffs so far are expected to show any impact on the data yet but should do so moving forward.

China will release key data for March, including first quarter GDP, on Thursday. A 5.3% year-on-year growth is expected, though this is before tariffs start impacting from the second quarter. For now, domestic activity seems steady, with retail sales and industrial production likely to grow by 4.5% and 6.0%, respectively. Fixed-asset investment is expected to stay mostly stable at 4.0%. Trade is slowing as tariffs take effect, but bigger impacts may come later.

Property prices will also be watched to see if market volatility has pushed them down further. Supporting domestic demand is the focus this year, though falling home prices make it hard to boost household confidence. While prices in major cities have stabilized, it’s unclear if this is the bottom.

Meanwhile, all attention is on how China will respond to the 145% tariffs imposed by Trump with a 125% tariff on US goods due to start on April 12. The market expects possible rate cuts, lower reserve requirement ratios (RRR), and new fiscal policies to support domestic demand.

In the Pacific region, we have some high-impact data from both Australia and New Zealand. On Tuesday, we have the RBA minutes, followed by Wednesday's New Zealand CPI release. The week ends on Thursday with employment data out of Australia.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US is enjoying a bit of a data break next week, but the tariff conversations will remain at the forefront. The eurozone and the UK have a bit more to offer on the data front next week, with the ECB interest rate meeting taking center stage.

The US is expected to have a calmer week due to Easter, but challenges remain. Tariffs have been delayed by 90 days, yet price hikes are still looming. Households are dealing with three key issues: higher prices reducing spending power, job loss fears due to federal budget cuts, and stock market drops making people cautious about major purchases.

March retail sales are likely to show strong growth as consumers rushed to buy before tariffs take effect. Auto sales rose 10.6%, and credit card data points to high demand for electronics and appliances. This may keep GDP growth positive in the first quarter, but future weakness is a concern.

Industrial output will be under the spotlight as Trump pushes for reshoring. However, unclear trade policies and fears of retaliation are making businesses nervous. Meanwhile, the Fed is expected to cut rates by around 90 basis points this year, with three or four 25bp cuts looking likely.

The rise in employers' national insurance could slow the job market, but so far, redundancy data hasn't increased. Watch for any drop in job vacancies or payroll numbers, though it seems unlikely.

Lower petrol prices may slightly reduce overall inflation in March, but services inflation is unlikely to fall much yet. More improvement could come in the second quarter if annual price adjustments are less steep. This is why the Bank of England may continue cutting rates each quarter through 2025 and into 2026.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

This week's focus remains on the US Dollar Index as it looks to recover from levels last seen in 2022.

The DXY has pushed below the psychological 100 barrier as well for the first time since July 2023. The last time the DXY breached this level, the move proved short-lived with the DXY going on an extended rally to the upside thereafter.

Given the dynamics at play this time around, such a move sees unlikely. However, a push back above the 100.00 barrier cannot be ruled out.

Given the speed of the selloff this week, coupled with the RSI period-14 hovering in oversold territory and the downside wick on Friday, a bounce cannot be ruled out.

Immediate resistance rests at 100.00 before the 100.617 and 101.18 handles come into focus.

If the selloff continues next week, immediate support rests at 99.57 and 99.00. A break of the 90.00 mark could result in a swift move lower toward the 97.70 support handle.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart - April 11, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 99.57
  • 99.00
  • 97.70

Resistance

  • 100.00
  • 100.61
  • 101.18
