data.title: TQQQ: A Contrarian Mag 7 ETF Buy
data.description:
The ProShares UltraPro QQQ (NASDAQ:TQQQ) has experienced a double-digit valuation correction this year as markets have experienced significant declines by concerns over an escalating trade war. In my opinion, the drop in valuations, especially in the big tech group, is a buying opportunity for investors that want to start to build long-term exposure to the fast-growing technology market which is being fueled by unprecedented gains in artificial intelligence. With AI adoption only going to rise going forward and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) reporting massive revenue growth in Q1'25, I believe the drop in the tech sector creates a great entry point for long-term investors into the ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

Previous rating

I rated the ProShares UltraPro QQQ a buy in my previous work on the ETF -- A Leveraged ETF Bet On Mag 7 Stocks -- because it was heavily over-weight ‘magnificent 7’ stocks that were set to benefit, in the long term, from growing investments in AI technology. With tech stocks really getting punished in the last several weeks, I believe that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a highly promising investment on the drop.

Leveraged bet on cheap magnificent 7 stocks

ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a leveraged bet on the tech sector as the ETF seeks to 3X the daily performance of its underlying benchmark, the Nasdaq-100 index. The recent market turmoil, related to the U.S. announcing new tariffs last week, has made leading tech franchises massively cheaper, with companies like Alphabet (GOOG) trading at just 16X forward (FY 2026) earnings.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ’s portfolio is dominated by magnificent 7 stocks, which are the seven most influential and powerful technology firms listed on U.S. stock exchanges. This group includes Alphabet, Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT).

The highest weighting in the ProShares UltraPro QQQ goes to Microsoft with a representation of 7.4%, followed by Apple (7.2%) and Nvidia (6.6%). Google is included in the top ten investments with a share of 3.4% because of TQQQ’s investment in Google Class A shares. However, the ETF also owns Google Class C shares (not included in the top ten holdings) which brings the ETF’s combined Alphabet exposure to 3.7%. In total, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ's top ten holdings represented 42.6% of the ETF's investments, which amounted to $24.8B on a net asset value basis.

All magnificent 7 stocks have material exposure to the theme of artificial intelligence, which has already been dominating investors' minds for at least the last 2–3 years. With AI adoption set to rise going forward, TQQQ's leading portfolio holdings (the top ten constituents broken down above) are set for sustained earnings and free cash flow growth.

Chipmaker TSMC just released preliminary Q1'25 revenue, which surged 42% compared to the year-earlier period due to a continual AI boom in the (server) market. Additionally, Gartner recently made a prediction that called for a massive 76% year-over-year increase in AI spending FY 2025, fueled by generative AI services.

In addition, the temporary suspension of tariffs, announced only a week ago, has led to a massive rebound in technology stocks on Wednesday. Since magnificent 7 stocks especially has seen dramatic valuation draw-downs recently, I believe that an entry into the technology sector could make a lot of sense, especially when considering that 1) Tech stocks are still quite cheap, and 2) The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has delivered impressive annual NAV returns since inception.

Impressive annual returns and long term growth potential

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a leveraged bet on magnificent 7 stocks and because of the strong performance of U.S. large-cap tech companies in the last decade, TQQQ has an impressive return profile: the ETF has achieved an annual NAV return of 38.83% since inception (the ProShares UltraPro QQQ was launched on February 9, 2010). With AI being a multi-year investment theme, I believe the current correction in the tech market is a unique opportunity to build exposure to ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

TQQQ’s valuation

Tech stocks have collapsed in the last several days, before benefiting from a massive rally on Wednesday after it was announced that the U.S. president paused previously announced tariffs for 90 days (with the exception of China). The Nasdaq-100, the underlying benchmark of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, bounced back especially hard on Wednesday, gaining 10%.

This rebound, in my opinion, is going to continue considering how cheap magnificent 7 stocks have become. If the U.S. reaches more trade deals with other countries, negative sentiment overhang could dissipate and clear the route for a new up-leg in the tech sector.

All magnificent 7 stocks, as an example, are still very much expected to see double-digit earnings growth in the years ahead and are trading at forward P/E ratios between 15-25X... which is bargain territory, in my opinion. Google, as an example, is trading at only 16.0X forward earnings which calculates to a 6%+ earnings yield and the company is expected to grow its earnings 16% annually in the long term. Google is one of the best deals in the market right now, in my opinion: Why I Am Going All-In Now.

It should be noted that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ employs leverage which has resulted, given the long term out-performance of large-cap U.S. technology companies, to highly impressive annual NAV returns. Since its inception, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has increased its net asset value by 2,230%, out-matching other leveraged ETF investment options like the Direxion Daily Technology Bull 3x Shares (TECL) by a massive margin.

Risks with TQQQ

I see two major risks for the ProShares UltraPro QQQ: 1) The ETF uses leverage to magnify its returns. Therefore, in a down-market, and especially in a market that punishes technology companies, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has a material chance to seriously under-perform the market, 2) The ETF is a sector-specific investment which investor are going to miss out on any kind of upside related to non-tech sectors going forward.

Final thoughts

The drop in valuations in the U.S. tech sector has led to a massive decline in the value of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF: the fund has dropped from $90 in February to less than $40 this week, before bouncing back 35% on Wednesday to $52. The year-to-date return is also looking weak: TQQQ is down 33% so far in FY 2025 which reflects the risks of a leveraged, magnificent 7-focused ETF. I believe, however, given that AI trends in the market are long term and that TSMC's Q1'25 revenue update indicates that the AI boom is not slowing down, that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has massive rebound potential. In my opinion, the drop constitutes a very interesting engagement opportunity and I see a highly favorable risk profile for long term investors that understand the risks and are capable of dealing with a high amount of volatility.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed-Income Monitor: April 2025
data.description:
By Katherine Nuss, CFA

Our proprietary Fixed-Income Monitor compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income. It’s designed to help investors identify opportunities and risks in the asset class.

Key takeaways for April 2025

  • Rising uncertainty related to escalating trade tensions fueled investor anxiety during March, sending risky assets lower in a flight to quality. High-quality fixed income is working as an effective risk hedge, with U.S. Treasury prices positive for the month and up over equities by more than 6% year to date.
  • Multiple measures of consumer and business sentiment collapsed during the month, highlighting the potential risks to economic growth from the implementation of broad-based tariffs. As a result, credit spreads in the most economically sensitive segments of the bond market widened. While this has created somewhat more attractive valuations, spreads remain tight to levels established during the growth scare of August 2024.
  • Municipal bonds lagged both Treasuries and investment-grade corporate bonds on challenging season patterns and the above-mentioned policy uncertainties.

Interpreting yields and spreads

One way to identify opportunities in fixed income is to look at bond yields. That’s because yield, which is based on a bond's price and coupon payments, reflects total return potential. Yields can change over time and across bond sectors. Spread, which refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries),1 indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk. If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are being paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less.

Disclosures

1 There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. Income from tax-exempt municipal bonds or municipal bond funds may be subject to state and local taxes, and a portion of income may be subject to the federal and/or state alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Federal and state income tax rules will apply to any capital gains.

Additional Disclosures

© 2016-2025 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Use of products, materials and services available through Columbia Threadneedle Investments may be subject to approval by your home office.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Funds and Columbia Acorn Funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA. Columbia Funds are managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC and Columbia Acorn Funds are managed by Columbia Wanger Asset Management, LLC, a subsidiary of Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC. ETFs are distributed by ALPS Distributors, Inc., member FINRA, an unaffiliated entity.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.
